meta.purpose: Factual source cards for protocol, communication, and industrial IoT concepts used in IoTEdges SEO content generation.

field_definitions.title: Topic name for the factual card.
field_definitions.primary_sources: Preferred sources to use when fact-checking content.
field_definitions.core_facts: Short factual claims that may be reused in page briefs and articles.
field_definitions.content_guardrails: Writing rules that prevent overclaiming or technical inaccuracies.
field_definitions.product_claim_checks: Product capability items that must be verified before making IoTEdges-specific claims.
field_definitions.supports_pages: SEO topic IDs or page themes supported by this card.
field_definitions.last_verified: Date this source card was last reviewed.

cards.title: MQTT
core_facts: MQTT is an OASIS standard messaging protocol commonly used for IoT messaging.
MQTT uses a publish/subscribe messaging model with clients publishing messages to topics and subscribers receiving messages through a broker.
MQTT defines Quality of Service levels commonly described as QoS 0, QoS 1, and QoS 2.
MQTT application messages include a topic name, payload data, QoS, and related properties in MQTT 5.0.
MQTT security design is implementation-specific; TLS, authentication, authorization, and credential management must be designed for the deployment context.
content_guardrails: Do not claim MQTT is automatically secure; explain that security depends on TLS, authentication, broker configuration, and deployment design.
Do not imply QoS 2 is always best; describe practical tradeoffs for telemetry volume, latency, and reliability.
Use MQTT 5.0 and MQTT 3.1.1 wording carefully if product version support is unknown.

cards.title: Modbus
core_facts: Modbus is an industrial communication protocol originally developed for exchanging process data between industrial control devices.
The Modbus application protocol is commonly used with serial links such as RS485 and with Ethernet/TCP networks.
Modbus RTU is commonly associated with serial communication, while Modbus TCP is used over TCP/IP networks.
Common Modbus data areas include coils, discrete inputs, input registers, and holding registers.
Modbus implementations require correct device addressing, function codes, register mapping, data type handling, and polling intervals.
content_guardrails: Do not treat Modbus as a physical layer; clarify that it is an application protocol that can run over different transports.
Do not publish device-specific register maps unless they are verified from the target device vendor.
Avoid claiming universal compatibility; Modbus projects often require register, data type, byte order, and scaling validation.
product_claim_checks: Maximum device count per serial bus.
Register mapping, scaling, and data type support.

cards.title: RS485 / TIA-485
core_facts: RS485, also referred to as TIA-485 or EIA-485, defines electrical characteristics for balanced serial communication.
RS485 is commonly used in industrial multi-drop serial networks and is often paired with Modbus RTU.
RS485 design depends on wiring topology, cable quality, termination, biasing, grounding, baud rate, and electrical noise conditions.
RS485 itself does not define an application protocol, register model, connector, or data payload format.
content_guardrails: Do not call RS485 a protocol; describe it as an electrical serial interface/standard.
Avoid fixed distance claims without explaining speed, cable, topology, and environment tradeoffs.
Do not prescribe unsafe wiring practices; keep troubleshooting advice practical and conservative.
product_claim_checks: Maximum practical device count in target deployment.

cards.title: LoRaWAN
core_facts: LoRaWAN is an LPWAN networking protocol maintained by the LoRa Alliance.
LoRaWAN is designed for low-power, long-range IoT networks with end devices, gateways, network servers, and application integrations.
LoRa is the radio modulation technology, while LoRaWAN defines the networking protocol and system behavior.
LoRaWAN deployments depend on regional parameters, gateway placement, antenna design, device class, payload size, and duty-cycle or regional regulatory limits.
content_guardrails: Do not use LoRa and LoRaWAN interchangeably.
Avoid fixed range promises; explain that coverage depends heavily on environment, antenna, height, obstacles, and regional settings.
Do not imply LoRaWAN is suitable for high-bandwidth or low-latency control loops.
product_claim_checks: Backhaul options such as Ethernet, WiFi, or cellular.

cards.title: OPC UA

primary_sources.name: OPC Unified Architecture Part 1: Overview and Concepts

core_facts: OPC UA is a platform-independent service-oriented architecture specification from the OPC Foundation.
OPC UA provides a common infrastructure for information modeling, messaging, communication, and interoperability.
OPC UA is used across industrial domains, including device-level, control-system, enterprise, IIoT, and machine-to-machine contexts.
OPC UA can represent structure, behavior, and semantics through its information model.
content_guardrails: Do not claim OPC UA support unless the product capability is confirmed.
Do not position OPC UA and MQTT as mutually exclusive in every scenario; they can be complementary in industrial architectures.
Avoid implying all PLCs or field devices natively support OPC UA.
product_claim_checks: Mapping from OPC UA nodes to MQTT or cloud payloads.

cards.title: 4G LTE
core_facts: LTE is part of the 3GPP family of mobile communication technologies commonly associated with 4G cellular networks.
Industrial cellular IoT deployments depend on carrier coverage, SIM provisioning, APN settings, signal quality, antenna design, and data plan constraints.
Cellular gateways are commonly used when wired Ethernet is unavailable or impractical at remote sites.
Cellular connectivity does not remove the need for secure application-layer protocols, authentication, and network configuration.
content_guardrails: Do not promise coverage in a country or region without carrier and band validation.
Do not treat LTE, LTE-M, NB-IoT, and 5G as interchangeable.
Avoid uptime claims without field data or service-level context.
product_claim_checks: MQTT reconnect and buffering behavior over cellular.

cards.title: RTU
core_facts: An RTU is commonly used in industrial control and remote monitoring systems to interface field devices with supervisory systems such as SCADA.
RTUs commonly collect analog and digital field data from sensors, meters, switches, and equipment status signals.
RTUs may communicate over wired, serial, Ethernet, radio, or cellular networks depending on design and product capability.
RTUs are often used in remote or unmanned sites where telemetry, alarms, and control signals must be sent to a central system.
content_guardrails: Do not imply every RTU is suitable for safety-critical control.
Do not claim support for DNP3, IEC 60870, IEC 61850, or other utility protocols unless confirmed.
Separate general RTU explanations from IoTEdges-specific product specs.
product_claim_checks: Alarm handling and data logging behavior.

cards.title: Remote IO
core_facts: Remote IO modules are used to collect or control field signals away from a central controller or gateway.
Common IO categories include digital input, digital output, analog input, analog output, and relay output.
Remote IO modules commonly communicate with controllers, RTUs, gateways, or SCADA systems using protocols such as Modbus RTU, Modbus TCP, or vendor-specific protocols.
Digital inputs are commonly used for status, alarm, pulse, or contact signals; analog inputs are commonly used for measurements such as level, pressure, temperature, and flow.
content_guardrails: Do not imply Remote IO always includes independent control logic; distinguish IO expansion from RTU or edge controller behavior.
Do not claim an IO module supports every sensor type without checking signal input specifications.
For analog content, distinguish 4-20mA, 0-10V, RTD, thermocouple, pulse, and digital contact inputs.
product_claim_checks: Relay ratings and safe switching limits.

cards.title: SCADA
core_facts: SCADA stands for supervisory control and data acquisition.
SCADA systems are used in industrial and infrastructure contexts where remote monitoring, supervisory control, data integrity, and communication over varied media are important.
RTUs, PLCs, communication networks, servers, and operator interfaces can all be part of SCADA architectures.
SCADA remote monitoring content should distinguish supervisory visibility from local machine control and safety functions.
content_guardrails: Do not claim IoTEdges replaces a full SCADA platform unless the product offering explicitly includes SCADA software.
Describe IoTEdges devices as data acquisition, telemetry, gateway, or field connectivity components when appropriate.
Avoid safety-critical or regulatory claims without system-level validation.
product_claim_checks: Integration interface such as MQTT, Modbus TCP, REST, or platform connector.

cards.title: Edge Computing in Industrial IoT

primary_sources.name: OPC Unified Architecture Part 1: Overview and Concepts

core_facts: Industrial edge computing commonly refers to processing, filtering, converting, or acting on industrial data closer to machines and field devices.
Edge gateways or controllers may collect data from protocols such as Modbus, OPC UA, or IO signals and forward selected data to cloud systems through MQTT or other interfaces.
Common edge functions include protocol conversion, local rules, data filtering, buffering, alarm handling, and reducing upstream data volume.
Actual edge capability depends entirely on product hardware, firmware, software, and supported integrations.
content_guardrails: Do not imply all gateways are edge controllers.
Do not claim AI, predictive maintenance, local control, or offline operation unless confirmed.
When discussing predictive maintenance, frame IoTEdges as data acquisition and integration unless analytics capability is confirmed.
